coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots synthesized with binary dopants for “turn off” and “turn off-on” sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lu-Shuang
Jiao, Xin-Yue
Zhang, Yu
Cheng, Cheng
Huang, Kun
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, highly fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by using citric acid as the carbon source with the binary dopants, i.e. melamine and formaldehyde, named as MF-CDs. The co-dopants played a crucial role to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of the MF-CDs, reaching 48%, in which melamine was the major nitrogen source, while formaldehyde was the co-dopant to regulate the state of nitrogen in the MF-CDs. The fluorescence of the MF-CDs was found to be quenched by Hg2+, and then recovered equally by individual halogen ions (eg. Cl−, Br− and I−). Correspondingly, the “turn off” and “turn off-on” sensing systems were separately established to detect Hg2+ and total halogen ions with good accuracy and reliability. In addition, the fluorescence of the MF-CDs was quenched by ClO−, and recovered by Fe2+; the “turn off” sensor was established to detect ClO− in tap water with low limit of detection of 0.18 μM, and the “turn off-on” sensor was to determine the ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets. Furthermore, owing to the low cytotoxicity, the MF-CDs were successful for cellular labeling on HepG2 cells. The developed binary dopants were effective to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield and endow the MF-CDs with some special properties, which would help to widen the applicability of the carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Turn off-on
Turn off
Binary dopants
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of Hg2+ and “turn off-on” sensing of halogen ions
Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of ClO− and “turn off-on” sensing of Fe2+
Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of Hg2+ and “turn off-on” sensing of halogen ions
Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of ClO− and “turn off-on” sensing of Fe2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31471208 & 31671195 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017KFYXJJ149 ). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of MF-CDs. Inset: high-resolution TEM image of MF-CDs. (b) SAED image of MF-CDs. (c) Size distribution of MF-CDs. (d) XRD pattern of MF-CDs. (e) FTIR spectrum of MF-CDs. (f) XPS full scan spectrum of MF-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of MF-CDs. Inset: the photographs of MF-CDs under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of MF-CDs with excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm by an increment of 10 nm. (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra of MF-CDs. (d) The fluorescence decay curve of MF-CDs (330 nm excitation, 410 nm emission).
(a) Selectivity of MF-CDs for Hg2+ over other ions (Xn±) and the coexisting interference of them. (b) Selectivity of MF-CDs for ClO− over other ions (Xn±) and the coexisting interference of them.

(a) The relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Hg2+ from 0 μM to 200 μM. Inset: fluorescence spectra of the MF-CDs solutions upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+. (b) A linear relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Hg2+ from 0.2 μM to 50 μM. Fluorescence spectra of the MF-CDs-Hg2+ solutions upon addition of various concentrations of (c) Cl−, (d) Br− and (e) I−. (f) The linear relationships between F/F0 and the concentration of respective halogen ions from 0.5 μM to 100 μM and average linearity curve of them (X−).

(a) The relationship between F/F0 and concentration of ClO− from 0 μM to 80 μM. Inset: fluorescence spectra of the MF-CDs solutions upon addition of various concentrations of ClO−. (b) The relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Fe2+ from 0 μM to 40 μM. Inset: fluorescence spectra of the MF-CDs-ClO− solutions upon addition of various concentrations of Fe2+. (c) The linear plot with the concentration of ClO− from 0.6 μM to 20 μM. (d) The linear plot with the concentration of Fe2+ from 1 μM to 20 μM.
Confocal microscopic images of HepG2 cells labeled with MF-CDs with different excitation wavelengths, (a) 488 nm, (b) 405 nm and (c) 559 nm (d) bright field, respectively.
Comparison of MF-CDs sensor with other CDs sensors for Hg2+ detection.

row.entry: CDs derived from citric acid and diethylenetriamine
CDs from pigeon feathers, egg and manure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Hg2+ in river water.
Comparison of MF-CDs sensor with other CDs sensors for ClO− detection.

row.entry: CDs prepared from citric acid and urea

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of ClO− in tap water.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots synthesized with binary dopants for “turn off” and “turn off-on” sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, highly fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by using citric acid as the carbon source with the binary dopants, i.e. melamine and formaldehyde, named as MF-CDs. The co-dopants played a crucial role to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of the MF-CDs, reaching 48%, in which melamine was the major nitrogen source, while formaldehyde was the co-dopant to regulate the state of nitrogen in the MF-CDs. The fluorescence of the MF-CDs was found to be quenched by Hg2+, and then recovered equally by individual halogen ions (eg. Cl−, Br− and I−). Correspondingly, the “turn off” and “turn off-on” sensing systems were separately established to detect Hg2+ and total halogen ions with good accuracy and reliability. In addition, the fluorescence of the MF-CDs was quenched by ClO−, and recovered by Fe2+; the “turn off” sensor was established to detect ClO− in tap water with low limit of detection of 0.18 μM, and the “turn off-on” sensor was to determine the ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets. Furthermore, owing to the low cytotoxicity, the MF-CDs were successful for cellular labeling on HepG2 cells. The developed binary dopants were effective to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield and endow the MF-CDs with some special properties, which would help to widen the applicability of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging class of carbonaceous materials with the size under 10 nm, were firstly discovered in 2004 [1]. Owing to their super properties, e.g. chemical inertness, resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [2], CDs were successfully applied in a variety of fields, such as fluorescent ink, imaging, sensors, catalysis, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices [3,4], etc. For diverse application purposes, CDs with high fluorescent quantum yield (QY) and specific physical and chemical properties were highly desired.

Various carbon precursors including chemical and natural sources [5–8] were adopted to generate CDs via different approaches [9]. It was reported that the introduction of nitrogen containing compound as the passivating agent could significantly enhance the QY of CDs [10]. Both the content and the chemical state of the doped nitrogen would affect the properties of the resulting CDs [11]. Therefore, to enrich categories of CDs with high QY and specific properties, further studies were necessary to find more efficient passivating agents.

In this study, melamine and formaldehyde were firstly explored as the binary dopants for the CDs originated from citric acid. Citric acid with abundant carboxylic groups was widely used as carbon precursor for CDs with good fluorescence, high water solubility and multiple functional groups [4,6,10]. Melamine, containing a high content of nitrogen, was an ideal nitrogen precursor. To regulate the chemical state of nitrogen in CDs to achieve a better fluorescent property, formaldehyde, which could react with melamine, was attempted as the co-dopant with melamine. The QY of the obtained CDs, named as MF-CDs, via hydrothermal method reached up to 48%, higher than those of CDs from the single dopant of melamine or formaldehyde, demonstrating the key role of binary dopants. The fluorescence of the MF-CDs was found to be quenched directly by mercuric ion (Hg2+) and hypochlorite (ClO−), and then recovered by halogen ions (Cl−/Br−/I−) and ferrous ion (Fe2+), respectively. Hence, two “turn off” and the corresponding “turn off-on” systems were established as illustrated in Fig. S1. Additionally, the MF-CDs showed low cytotoxicity and were successfully applied in cell imaging.

Anhydrous citric acid was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Melamine, formaldehyde solution (37.0–40.0%, about 14 M), NaNO3, KNO3, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NiSO4·6H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, NaCl, KCl, KBr, KI, NaAcO·3H2O, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, NaNO2, Na3PO4·12H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) aqueous solution were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). The human hepatoma cell line (HepG2) was obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). The ferrous succinate tablets were purchased from a local drugstore (Wuhan, China). The river water was collected from the Han River (Wuhan, China).

MF-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. For a typical process, 1.92 g (10 mmol) citric acid and 0.0315 g (0.25 mmol) melamine were firstly dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 560 μL (8 mmol) formaldehyde solution. The solution was well mixed and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 220 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the resulted deep yellow solution was filtrated through a 0.22 μm membrane and the filtrate was further dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis bag (1000 Da molecular weight cut off) for 24 h with renewing the water every 6 h to remove the small molecules. Afterwards, by evaporating the solution inside the dialysis bag and drying at 80 °C, the MF-CDs powder was obtained.

Quinine sulfate with a QY of 54% at 360 nm was used as the reference to calculate the QY of CDs. Quinine sulfate and CDs were dissolved at a series of concentrations in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index η = 1.33) and ultrapure water (η = 1.33) respectively for absorbance and fluorescence measurements at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. In order to minimize the self-absorption effect, the absorbance of each solution was below 0.1. The QY of MF-CDs was calculated by the following Eq. (1),
(1) QY = QYR·(k/kR)·(η/ηR)2
where k is the slope of the linear fitted curve with integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance; η is the solvent refractive index. The subscript R stands for reference (quinine sulfate).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) images were captured by a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected on an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence spectra and intensity were measured on an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis spectra were obtained by a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded on a Fluorescence lifetime spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, USA).

To assess pH stability, the pH of the MF-CDs solution was adjusted by HCl or NaOH to a prescribed value (1–13). As for ionic strength stability, NaCl, a commonly-used salt, was introduced into the MF-CDs solution at the final concentration from 0 to 1.0 M. The fluorescence spectra and intensity of the resultant solutions were then recorded at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The MF-CDs solution was continuously irradiated at the wavelength of 330 nm for 2 h, during which the fluorescence intensity was monitored every minute to evaluate the photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of Hg2+ and “turn off-on” sensing of halogen ions
ce:section.ce:para: For “turn off” sensing of Hg2+, 300 μL of PBS buffer solution (0.2 M NaH2PO4-H3PO4, pH 3.0), 200 μL of MF-CDs solution (40 μg/mL) and 1500 μL of Hg2+ working solution at a prescribed concentration (0–200 μM) or sample solution were mixed in a 4 mL Eppendorf tube, and then ultrapure water was added to make the volume up to 3 mL. After being vortex mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature, the solution was transferred to a quartz cell of 10 mm path length for fluorescence measurement. The excitation and emission wavelengths were fixed at 330 nm and 410 nm respectively throughout the experiment. The F/F0 was calculated, where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs solution in the presence and absence of Hg2+ (or other ions in the following selectivity experiment and the evaluation of coexisting effect), respectively. For selectivity experiment, 1500 μL of the specific ion solution at the concentration of 50 μM instead of Hg2+ was introduced to the MF-CDs solution. To evaluate the coexisting effect, different ions together with Hg2+ (50 μM) were simultaneously introduced to the MF-CDs solution and their interference on the quench of fluorescence (F/F0) was assessed.

The established sensing system was applied to the detection of Hg2+ in river water. The silver nitrate was added to the river water sample at the concentration of 500 μM to remove the potential interfering ions (e.g. SO3 2−, Cl−, Br− and I−) before analysis. The recovery test was carried out by spiking a specific amount of Hg2+ into the river water and the ratio of the measured to the added concentration of Hg2+ was calculated. The river water was also analyzed by a standard method using the DMA-80 Direct Mercury Analyzer (Milestone Srl., Italy) for comparison.

For “turn off-on” detection of halogen ions, 300 μL of PBS buffer solution (0.2 M NaH2PO4-H3PO4, pH 3.0), 200 μL of MF-CDs solution (40 μg/mL) and a specific amount of Hg2+ (equivalent to 50 μM in the 1500 μL sample solution) were mixed together, which was denoted as MF-CDs-Hg2+. Halogen ions solution at a prescribed concentration (0–100 μM, 1500 μL) was then added and ultrapure water was used to dilute the solution to a total volume of 3 mL. After being vortex mixed and incubated for 10 min, the solution was subjected to fluorescence measurement. The F/F0 was calculated, where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs-Hg2+ solution with and without the halogen ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of ClO− and “turn off-on” sensing of Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: For “turn off” detection of ClO−, 300 μL of PBS buffer solution (0.2 M NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4, pH 6.0), 200 μL of MF-CDs solution (40 μg/mL) and 1500 μL of ClO− working solution at a prescribed concentration (0–80 μM) or sample solution were added in a 4 mL Eppendorf tube and ultrapure water was added to make the volume up to 3 mL. After being vortex mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature, the solution was subjected to fluorescence measurement. The ratio (F/F0) of fluorescence intensity of the solution with and without ClO− was calculated. The selectivity and coexisting interference were evaluated in the same way as Section 2.6.1. The developed method was applied to detect ClO− in tap water collected in our laboratory without any pretreatment. For the recovery test, the tap water spiked with the specific amount of ClO− was analyzed.

For “turn off-on” detection of Fe2+, 300 μL of PBS buffer solution (0.2 M NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4, pH 6.0), 200 μL of MF-CDs solution (40 μg/mL) and a specific amount of ClO− (equivalent to 20 μM in the 1500 μL sample solution) were mixed together, which was denoted as MF-CDs-ClO−. Then 1500 μL of Fe2+ working solution at different concentrations (0–40 μM) or sample solution was added, and ultrapure water was used to dilute the solution to a total volume of 3 mL. After being vortex mixed and incubated for 10 min, the solution was subjected to fluorescence measurement, and the F/F0 was calculated, where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the MF-CDs-ClO− solution with and without Fe2+, respectively.

The established “turn off-on” system was applied to quantify ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets. The pretreatment of the tablets was carried out according to Chinese Pharmacopoeia with a little modification [12]. Briefly, the tablet was ground into powder after removing the coating and then 1 g tablet powder was dissolved in the mixture of 65 mL water and 15 mL H2SO4 (1 M) with the addition of 0.75 g zinc powder. The solution was filtrated through a vertical melting funnel paved with a thin layer of zinc powder, and the filtrate was diluted to 100 mL with ultrapure water for analysis. For comparison, the ferrous succinate was also quantified by the titration method according to Chinese Pharmacopoeia [12].

HepG2 cells were randomly selected as represents for cell imaging and cytotoxicity study. The HepG2 cells with a certain density were seeded to 12-well plates with a cover glass at the bottom and cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing 10% fatal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C and 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Then the cells were cultured in DMEM medium with MF-CDs at 20 μg/mL for 1 h. Afterwards, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS (NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4-NaCl, pH 7.3) to remove the physically adsorbed MF-CDs. Thus MF-CDs labeled cells were observed under the laser excitation at 405, 488 and 559 nm by a confocal microscopy (Olympus FV1200, Japan).

MTT assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of the MF-CDs. The HepG2 cells were seeded and cultured in a 96-well plate containing DMEM medium without MF-CDs for 24 h, and then with the addition of MF-CDs of a series of concentrations (0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100 and 500 μg/mL) for another 24 h. The MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well to form formazan, which was then dissolved by 100 μL of DMSO. The absorbance of the solution in each well was measured by a microplate reader (Biorad, USA) with the excitation wavelength of 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated by the following Eq. (2):
(2) cell viability (%) = (A/ABlank) × 100%
where A and ABlank represented the absorbance of the well solution with the cells treated with and without MF-CDs in the DMEM medium, respectively.

In this study, hydrothermal method was used to generate MF-CDs with citric acid as major carbon source. The nitrogen-rich compound, melamine, was selected as the nitrogen source, while formaldehyde, which can react with melamine [13], was simultaneously added into the synthesis system as the cooperative passivating agent to possibly regulate the chemical state of nitrogen in MF-CDs and thus enhance the QY. The polymerization, dehydration and carbonization may be involved in this hydrothermal process [14]. The amounts of the passivating agents, reaction temperature and time may have influence on the properties of the resulting MF-CDs [15], and were optimized by one variant one time. As seen from Fig. S2(a), the QY first sharply increased and then gradually decreased along with the increasing addition of melamine, which illustrates the introduction of appropriate amount of nitrogen dopant was beneficial to enhance the QY. From Fig. S2(b), the QY of the MF-CDs reached a plateau with the increasing formaldehyde amount up to 8 mmol; the maximum QY compared to the lowest one without the addition of formaldehyde was about 25% enhancement, which fully demonstrates the cooperative role of formaldehyde to enhance the QY. In the preliminary experiment, the melamine-formaldehyde resin was also attempted as the dopant instead of respective melamine and formaldehyde, but lower QY was obtained. The reason might be that formaldehyde and melamine may separately react with citric acid, and meanwhile they may undergo polymerization in parallel with citric acid, thus achieving more effective emissive sites on the MF-CDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S2(c) and (d), the reaction temperature and time were found to have little effect on the QY of the MF-CDs in the range we tested and the highest QY was obtained at 220 °C and 10 h. The optimized synthetic conditions were described in Section 2.2.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), the MF-CDs were monodispersed and quasi spherical in shape. The size of the MF-CDs was in the range of 4–10 nm and centered at 7 nm (Fig. 1(c)). According to the SAED pattern (Fig. 1(b)) and the close observation of MF-CDs (Fig. 1(a) inset), a lattice spacing at 0.21 nm was observed, correlated well with the broad peak at 22° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1(d)), revealing the crystalline structure of MF-CDs [16–19].

FTIR and XPS were employed to characterize the surface functional groups and chemical composition of the MF-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1(e), a broad absorption band at 3412 cm−1 was attributed to OH/NH stretching vibration [20]. The absorption peaks at 2957 cm−1, 1699 cm−1, 1570 cm−1, 1417 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1 were related to CH, CO, CC, COO and CO/CN stretching vibration, respectively [21]. The FTIR spectrum reveals that multiple functionalities are present on the surface of MF-CDs, e.g. OH, COOH, NH2, etc., which would render the MF-CDs high hydrophilicity and favorable interaction sites with other substances for further applications [22].

In Fig. 1(f), the XPS full scan spectrum of MF-CDs presented three predominant peaks at 531.44 eV, 399.94 eV and 284.78 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of O 1s (24.57%), N 1s (2.41%) and C 1s (73.02%), respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S3(a)) consisted of four peaks at 284.39 eV, 285.09 eV, 286.00 eV and 288.36 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively [23]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S3 (b)) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.23 eV (CO) and 532.48 eV (COH/COC) [24]. Two subpeaks at 399.77 eV (NC) and 400.97 eV (NH) were obtained in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3(c)) [24]. The XPS results were consistent with FTIR analysis, demonstrating the multiple functionalities containing oxygen or nitrogen on the surface of MF-CDs.

The UV–vis absorbance spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)) of MF-CDs displayed a wide absorption band ranging from 200 nm to 400 nm without apparent peak, attributing to the n–π* transition [25]. The aqueous solution of MF-CDs was colorless and nearly transparent under visible light, and was blue under UV irradiation (Fig. 2(a) inset). Both the excitation and emission spectra of MF-CDs settled in a wide wavelength range (Fig. 2(a)). The excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior was also observed and the peak fluorescence was obtained at excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm (Fig. 2(b, c)). The fluorescence decay curve of MF-CDs was exhibited in Fig. 2(d). The MF-CDs contained three lifetime components, i.e. 12.97 ns (61.95%), 0.47 ns (6.14%) and 3.57 ns (31.91%), and the average lifetime was calculated to be 10.06 ns. The multi-exponential property of the lifetime indicates that different emissive sites may be present on MF-CDs [26].

The fluorescence stability of MF-CDs was illustrated in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs remained almost constant in a wide pH range from 2 to 10, and decreased almost unnoticeably in strong acidic or basic conditions (Fig. S4(a)). The MF-CDs also exhibited high resistance to ionic strength over the studied NaCl concentration range (Fig. S4(b)). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs remained unchanged after continuous irradiation at 330 nm for 2 h (Fig. S4(c)), reflecting the excellent photostability of MF-CDs. All these appreciated characters would add the application value of MF-CDs.

As discussed above, a lot of functional groups consisting of oxygen or nitrogen were present on the surface of MF-CDs, which were supposed to offer the interaction sites with target substances. In our preliminary experiment, the fluorescence of the prepared MF-CDs was selectively quenched by both Hg2+ and ClO−. Considering that the coexistence of Hg2+ and ClO− was rare, the respective detection of Hg2+ and ClO− could be possible based on the fluorescence quenching, and the corresponding “turn off-on” detection systems were also proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of Hg2+ and “turn off-on” sensing of halogen ions
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of Hg2+, the experimental conditions were optimized in terms of pH, buffer type, incubation time and temperature, as shown in Fig. S5. The fluorescence of CDs may arise from quantum confinement effects or the numerous emissive sites on the surface [2]. The fluorescence quenching caused by Hg2+ may be attributed to the binding of Hg2+ with the functional groups on the MF-CDs, e.g. OH and COOH, promoting non-radiative electron/hole recombination through the charge transfer process thus changing the surface state of MF-CDs [27]. At the optimal detection condition as described in Section 2.6.1, the fluorescence of the prepared MF-CDs was selectively quenched by only Hg2+ and ClO−, and unaffected by other ions (Fig. 3 (a)).

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the fluorescence of MF-CDs was gradually quenched with the increasing Hg2+ concentration. A good linear relationship was obtained between F/F0 and the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.2 μM–50 μM (Fig. 4(b)). The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was calculated to be 0.06 μM. Table 1 compares the sensitivity of our method and other reported CDs sensors for the detection of Hg2+. The LOD of our MF-CDs sensor was comparable or lower than those of others, and the linear range was also relatively wide, demonstrating it was a promising sensor for the detection of Hg2+.

The established detection system was applied to determine Hg2+ in river water. As seen from Fig. 3(a), coexisting ions like SO3 2−, Cl−, Br− and I− may interfere the fluorescence quenching by Hg2+. In order to eliminate the possible interference from these ions, the river water was first treated by silver nitrate to remove them. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries of Hg2+ were in the range of 98–107% with RSDs ≤ 6.2%, proving the accuracy and reliability of this assay. There was no Hg2+ detected in river water or the concentration of it may be lower than LOD of this method. Also, no Hg2+ was detected in the river water by the DMA-80 Direct Mercury Analyzer (with an LOD of 1 nM), further verifying the accuracy of our method.

The “turn off-on” phenomenon was observed upon the addition of respective halogen ion, as depicted in Fig. 4(c–e). The recovery of the fluorescence by halogen ions may be attributing to the stronger binding of them than MF-CDs with Hg2+ thus resulting in the release of MF-CDs and the restoration of the fluorescence [27]. In addition, some sulfhydryl small molecules which have high affinity with Hg2+, e.g. glutathione, cysteine and homocysteine, were also attempted in our experiment; this “turn off-on” phenomenon was observed as well. These results further verify the hypothesis of the quenching mechanism. Particularly, different with previous studies [27,33], the fluorescence of MF-CDs quenched by Hg2+ could be equally restored by not only I− but also Cl− and Br−, which may be attributing to the stronger affinity to Hg2+ of these three halogen ions than MF-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 4(f), the good linear relation was obtained between the F/F0 and the concentration of respective halogen ion in the range of 0.5 μM–100 μM. The correlation coefficients of the three regression equations were all higher than 0.99, and the slopes of the three linearity were almost the same, demonstrating almost the equivalent “turn on” efficiency by the three kinds of halogen ions. The average linearity curve of them could be calculated with a correlation coefficient of 0.9931. Hence, this method may be applicable to determine the total content of halogen ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” sensing of ClO− and “turn off-on” sensing of Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by ClO− may be different from that by Hg2+. During the synthetic process the incomplete carbonization may occur rendering some reductive hydroxyl groups on the surface of MF-CDs [34], which was also demonstrated by the above characterizations in Section 3.2. The oxidizing agent, ClO− may change the surface states of MF-CDs through the oxidative process thus leading to the quenching of fluorescence [34]. The optimization of fluorescence quenching condition for ClO− detection was carried out in the similar way to Section 3.3.1, and the results are depicted in Fig. S5. Although the reductive agents (e.g. Fe2+, SO3 2−, I−, Br−, NO2 −) could suppress the quenching effect to some degree as seen in Fig. 3(b), they may not coexist with ClO− in genuine samples. Thus, the interference of them could be ignored.

In Fig. 5 (a), the fluorescence of MF-CDs was gradually quenched with the increasing concentration of ClO−. The F/F0 was in line with the ClO− concentration range of 0.6 μM–20 μM (Fig. 5(c)), with an LOD of 0.18 μM. The sensitivity to ClO− of this method is moderate and comparable to some of other reported CDs probes, as listed in Table 3 , which is sufficient for the quantification of ClO− in tap water.The concentration of ClO− in tap water detected by this method was calculated to be 3.57 μM. As listed in Table 4 , the recoveries of ClO− were in the range of 99–111% with RSDs ≤ 6.2%, proving the accuracy and reliability of this method.

Some strong ruductive agents, e.g. S2−, ascorbic acid, glutathione and Fe2+, were found to recover the fluorescence of MF-CDs quenched by ClO−, possibly owing to the reductive property of them, which was the basis to establish the “turn off-on” sensor. Fe2+ was herein taken as an example to determine ferros succinate in tablets as a quality control application. As depicted in Fig. 5(b, d), F/F0 was in line with the concentration of Fe2+ in the range of 1 μM–20 μM with an LOD at 0.3 μM. The content of ferrous succinate calculated by this method (0.1035 ± 0.0042 g per tablet) was comparable to the result measured by the titration method (0.1036 ± 0.0003 g per tablet), and consistent with the labeled amount of ferrous succinate in the tablets (0.1 g per tablet), demonstrating the good accuracy and reliability of this method.

As shown in Fig. S6, MTT study revealed that the average cell viability was greater than 91% at MF-CDs concentration up to 500 μg/mL. This result suggests that the MF-CDs exerted low toxicity to HepG2 cells which would be potentially used for cell imaging or other biomedical applications.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the MF-CDs-labeled HepG2 cells may emit bright fluorescence when excited at 405, 488 and 559 nm. MF-CDs may easily penetrate the cell membrane thus labeling the cells [38]. The multicolor emission supplied a wide wavelength range for observation, thus making the MF-CDs a promising cellular imaging agent.

The carbon dots were obtained by hydrothermal method with citric acid as carbon source and the mixture of melamine and formaldehyde as the binary dopants, named as MF-CDs. The cooperative role of the melamine and formaldehyde was important to achieve the relatively high fluorescence quantum yield. The prepared MF-CDs possessed multiple functional groups and excellent fluorescence stability to pH, high ionic strength and UV irradiation. Two “turn off” sensors were established depending on the fluorescence quenching of the MF-CDs by Hg2+ and ClO−, and applied in the detection of them in river water and tap water, respectively. Furthermore, two “turn off-on” sensors were constructed on the basis that the fluorescence of MF-CDs quenched by Hg2+ and ClO− could be selectively recovered by halogen ions (Cl−/Br−/I−) and Fe2+ respectively. Additionally, due to the low cytotoxicity of the MF-CDs, it was successfully applied for cell imaging with multicolour emission on HepG2 cells. The present study not only provided a new strategy to enhance quantum yield of CDs, but also expanded the application range of the CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31471208 & 31671195) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017KFYXJJ149). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.189.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications

Carbon dots: materials, synthesis, properties and approaches to long-wavelength and multicolor emission
A review of carbon dots in biological applications

Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid

Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion

Bottom-up electrochemical preparation of solid-state carbon nanodots directly from nitriles/ionic liquids using carbon-free electrodes and the applications in specific ferric ion detection and cell imaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Lu-shuang Li is a PhD student in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Her research topic focuses on the synthesis of novel carbon dots for multiple applications.

Prof. Kun Huang is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. He received his PhD degree from Case Western Reserve University in 2006. His research interests focus on mechanistic study and therapy of metabolic diseases.

Prof. Li Xu is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD degree from National University of Singapore in 2009. Her research interests focus on separation and analysis of complex samples and application of functional materials in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.
